mcqs.question: In the context of Naive Bayes classification, what does the 'naive' assumption specifically refer to?

options.text: The classifier assumes all training examples are equally important
The classifier assumes features are conditionally independent given the class label
The classifier requires no prior probabilities to be specified
The classifier assumes a Gaussian distribution for all continuous features

mcqs.explanation: The 'naive' assumption refers to conditional independence of features given the class, meaning P(X₁,X₂,...,Xₙ|Y) = ∏P(Xᵢ|Y).

mcqs.question: In Bayes' theorem formula P(Y|X) = [P(X|Y) × P(Y)] / P(X), which term represents the posterior probability?

options.text: P(Y)
P(X|Y)
P(Y|X)
P(X)

mcqs.explanation: P(Y|X) is the posterior probability - the probability of class Y given observed features X.

mcqs.question: Consider a Naive Bayes classifier for spam detection. If the word 'winner' appears in 80% of spam emails and 10% of ham emails, with prior probabilities P(spam)=0.3 and P(ham)=0.7. Using Bayes theorem, what is P(spam|'winner') ignoring the denominator?
mcqs.explanation: P('winner'|spam) × P(spam) = 0.8 × 0.3 = 0.24. This is the unnormalized posterior (numerator of Bayes theorem).

mcqs.question: What is the primary purpose of applying Laplace (additive) smoothing in Naive Bayes classifiers?

options.text: To increase the accuracy of the classifier on test data
To prevent zero probabilities when a feature value does not occur with a particular class
To reduce overfitting by limiting model complexity
To convert categorical features to numerical format

mcqs.explanation: Laplace smoothing adds a small constant (typically 1) to count calculations to avoid zero probabilities for unseen feature-class combinations.

mcqs.question: Which Naive Bayes variant is most suitable for classification tasks involving binary feature vectors (e.g., word presence/absence in documents)?

options.text: Gaussian Naive Bayes
Multinomial Naive Bayes
Bernoulli Naive Bayes
Categorical Naive Bayes

mcqs.explanation: Bernoulli Naive Bayes is designed for binary/boolean features, modeling presence (1) or absence (0) of features.

mcqs.question: In Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimation for Naive Bayes, which of the following quantities is directly maximized to find the optimal class?

options.text: P(Features|Class)
P(Class|Features) ∝ P(Features|Class) × P(Class)
P(Features) × P(Class)
P(Class) / P(Features)

mcqs.explanation: MAP estimation maximizes the posterior probability P(Class|Features), which is proportional to P(Features|Class) × P(Class) by Bayes theorem.

mcqs.question: A medical diagnosis system uses Gaussian Naive Bayes with two symptoms (fever severity and cough intensity, both continuous). If a patient has high fever and severe cough, what is the computational approach used to calculate P(symptoms|disease)?

options.text: Multiplying the marginal probabilities of each symptom
Using the product of individual Gaussian probability density functions assuming conditional independence
Computing the joint probability distribution without any assumption
Using logistic regression to estimate the probability

mcqs.explanation: Gaussian NB assumes each feature follows a normal distribution within each class, and calculates P(X|Y) as the product of individual Gaussian PDFs.

mcqs.question: Which statement correctly explains a key limitation of the Naive Bayes classifier despite its widespread use in practice?

options.text: Naive Bayes cannot handle missing values in the dataset
The conditional independence assumption is often violated in real-world data, which can degrade performance
Naive Bayes always requires continuous features and cannot work with categorical data
It cannot be used for multi-class classification problems

mcqs.explanation: The conditional independence assumption rarely holds true in practice (features are often correlated), yet NB often performs well despite this violation.
